content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 4, 2004

Steven G. Churchwell
Livingston & Mattesich
1201 K Street, Suite 1100
Sacramento, CA 95814-3938

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-063

Dear Mr. Churchwell:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Richard Ferreira for advice regarding the post-governmental employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We encourage you to consider whether other laws, such as Government Code 1090 or the doctrine of incompatible offices, may also be implicated by your situation. The Commission is charged with interpreting and enforcing the provisions of the Act, and may provide advice only with respect to those provisions. (Section 83114.) Our advice is based on the facts presented in your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. Does the permanent ban on "switching sides" prevent Mr. Ferreira from performing as a consultant and possibly as an expert witness for the Kern County Water Agency ("KCWA") in a dispute concerning the Department of Water Resources' ("DWR") application of its guidelines for calculating revenues and costs from its hydroelectric facilities, for purposes of billing its water contractors, which were adopted as a result of the meetings of the Committee on Energy of the Water Service Contractor's Council ("Committee")?

2. May Mr. Ferreira provide consultation services to the KCWA concerning the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission's ("FERC") relicensing process and control of associated costs in connection with renewal of DWR's FERC license for its Hyatt-Thermalito facility?

_______________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04-063
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

1 & 2. Because Mr. Ferreira is still employed as a consultant and under a contract with
the California Consumer Power and Conservation Financing Authority, and is a
designated employee under its conflict of interest code, he has not permanently left state
service. Therefore, the post-employment provisions of the Act do not preclude Mr.
Ferreira from working as a consultant to KCWA. However, there may be conflict of
interest issues with respect to specific governmental decisions, depending on whether
KCWA determines that Mr. Ferreira is a "consultant" for purposes of disclosure under
the Act.

FACTS

Mr. Ferreira was employed by the Department of Water Resources ("DWR")
from 1964 to 1986. The Kern County Water Agency ("KCWA") would like to hire
Mr. Ferreira as a consultant and potential expert witness.

Mr. Ferreira's positions and duties with the DWR were as follows:

February 1964 to April 1976 – Electric Utilities Engineer. Supervised
and performed various engineering, economic and financial studies related to
generation and transmission planning and development.

May 1976 to April 1982 – Chief, Renewable Resource Development
Branch. Responsible for the permitting, licensing, management and
administration of alternative resource development, including hydroelectric, wind,
solar and other alternate energy sources. This branch did not administer water
supply contracts.

May 1982 to December 1986 – Chief, Power Contracts Branch.
Responsible for strategizing and directing the negotiations and administration of
multi-million dollar bulk power contracts and developing markets for the sale of
surplus power.

The provisions of the water supply contract between DWR and KCWA that are in
dispute were executed in 1963, prior to Mr. Ferreira joining the DWR. In January 1976,
the DWR and the local water districts (a.k.a. water service contractors) formed the
"Committee on Energy of the Water Service Contractor's Council." The director of the
DWR appointed the chief of the Energy Division, Edward Terhaar, to chair the
Committee. Issues of mutual concern to the state and the contractors were discussed at
these meetings. The Committee met quarterly, and Mr. Ferreira attended six such meetings
between March 8, 1976, and March 3, 1977.

As a result of these meetings, the DWR adopted in 1977 guidelines for how it
would calculate revenues and costs from its hydroelectric facilities for purposes of billing

File No. A-04-063
Page No. 3

its water contractors. These guidelines are the source of the current dispute between the
DWR and the KCWA.

Mr. Ferreira joined the Sacramento Municipal Utility District ("SMUD") in
January 1987 and retired in December 2000. While employed by SMUD, he also served
on the board of the California Independent System Operator ("ISO") from 1998 through
2000.

He also served as a consultant to the California Energy Resources Scheduling
Division ("CERS") of the DWR from January 31, 2001, through August 31, 2001, during
the period commonly known as the "California Energy Crisis." He currently provides
consulting services to the California Consumer Power and Conservation Financing
Authority ("CPA") pursuant to a contract that runs from September 12, 2002, through
September 11, 2005. You confirmed by telephone to staff that Mr. Ferreira's consulting
firm, Richard Ferreira, Inc., is comprised solely of Mr. Ferreira.

Mr. Ferreira currently is employed part-time at SMUD as an advisor to the
assistant general manager on electric restructuring matters and represents the
Transmission Agency of Northern California ("TANC") as a member of the board of the
Western Systems Coordinating Council ("WSCC").

In a telephone conversation with staff on April 28, 2004, you stated that
represents the
Mr. Ferreira files Statements of Economic Interests ("SEI") with both CPA and SMUD.
According to the contract between CPA and Richard Ferreira, Inc., which is attached to
your request for advice, CPA is a state agency.

Proposed Consulting Agreement with KCWA

There is a dispute between the KCWA and the DWR under a water supply
contract, executed in 1963, pursuant to which the DWR delivers water to the KCWA from
the state water project. The DWR bills the KCWA and other local water districts known as
state water contractors ("SWC") annually for delivered water. The bills include a
component known as the Delta Water Charge ("DWC").

KCWA contends that the DWR has been calculating the DWC incorrectly under
its water supply contract, because the DWR has not been crediting against the DWC the
full value of sales by the DWR of electrical energy from the DWR's hydro-electric
generating facilities at the Hyatt-Thermalito complex in Oroville, California. Mr.
Ferreira's proposed task as consultant is to supervise the auditing of energy sales and other
energy transactions and allocations of revenues derived from the Hyatt-Thermalito
complex and other DWR-owned generating resources for the years since 1980.

This information may already be available to the KCWA from the DWR in
summarized or tabulated form, in which event relatively less work in this area may be
required. If not, the collection of this information could require the review of a large
volume of documents related to energy sales transactions in order to identify the

File No. A-04-063
Page No. 4

generating source. Once this information is retrieved, it is intended to be used by the
KCWA in certain "damages" calculations intended to explain, to the DWR, to other state
water contractors, or to a trier of fact, how energy revenues are properly to be allocated to
the DWC under the DWR's water supply contracts. There is a possibility that Mr. Ferreira
would give expert testimony concerning the calculations of revenues attributable to
different generating resources in the state water project for the period January 1994 to the
present.

The proposed contract between KCWA and Mr. Ferreira contains the following
provision:

"III. Responsibility of Engineer:

"Engineer is retained as an independent contractor to render
a professional service only, and not as an employee of the
Agency. The Parties agree that Engineer shall only provide
advice, recommendations, and/or expert testimony to the
Agency and that Engineer shall have no role in the review
of decision-making process regarding Engineer's advice
and/or recommendations. The Parties further agree that the
Agency shall independently satisfy itself as to such advice
and/or recommendations and shall not act in sole reliance
upon the advice of the Engineer without independent
investigation or review. The Parties also agree that, as
between themselves, the Agency shall also bear
responsibility to undertake and secure all appropriate levels
of Agency approvals in the implementation of any of
Engineer's advice and/or recommendations."

Oroville Facility

The DWR operates the Hyatt-Thermalito facility under a license from FERC. The
current Oroville facility FERC license expires in 2007. The formal FERC relicensing
process for that facility began in 2002. In order to renew the FERC license, the DWR has
incurred and will incur significant costs between now and the end of the current license.
DWR is currently billing the state water contractors, including the KCWA, for these
FERC relicensing costs through the DWC.

The KCWA does not have a present dispute with the DWR over these FERC
relicensing costs. However, as a state water contractor, the KCWA has an obvious interest
in participating in the FERC relicensing process and in controlling these costs. Mr.
Ferreira's experience in FERC relicensing stems from his employment at SMUD in the
relicensing of SMUD generating facilities.

The KCWA desires Mr. Ferreira's consultation with regard to the FERC
relicensing process and the control of relicensing costs. His consultations with the KCWA

File No. A-04-063
Page No. 5

regarding the FERC relicensing do not relate to the past work that he performed while
employed by the DWR. It is not expected that his consultation will involve testimony
adverse to DWR in any adversary proceeding between the DWR and the KCWA.

ANALYSIS

1. Does the permanent ban on "switching sides" prevent Mr. Ferreira from
performing as a consultant and possibly as an expert witness for the Kern
County Water Agency in a dispute concerning the Department of Water
Resources' application of its guidelines for calculating revenues and costs
from its hydroelectric facilities, for purposes of billing its water
contractors, which were adopted as a result of the meetings of the
Committee on Energy of the Water Service Contractor's Council?

Revolving Door

Officials who leave state service are subject to two types of restrictions under the
Act. The first is a permanent ban, and the second is a one-year prohibition, commonly
called the "revolving door" provisions of the Act.

Sections 87401 and 87402 (collectively, the "permanent ban") prohibit former
state administrative officials from advising or representing any person for compensation in
any judicial or other proceeding in which the official participated while in state service.
(Sections 87401 and 87402.) Specifically, Section 87401 provides:

"No former state administrative official, after the
termination of his or her employment or term of office,
shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, or
otherwise represent, any other person (other than the State
of California) before any court or state administrative
agency or any officer or employee thereof by making any
formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or
written communication with the intent to influence, in
connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding if both of the following apply:

"(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and
substantial interest.

"(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state
administrative official participated."

"'State administrative official' means every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state administrative agency who as part of his or her official
responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other than a
purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity." (Section 87400(b).)

File No. A-04-063
Page No. 6

Regulation 18741.1, in pertinent part, further clarifies:

"(a) The prohibitions of Government Code Sections 87401
and 87402 apply to any state administrative official if all of
the following criteria are met:

"(1) The official has permanently left state service or is on
a leave of absence."

Because Mr. Ferreira is required to file a Statement of Economic Interests by
CPA, we may conclude that CPA has determined that he is a "consultant," as defined in
regulation 18702.1(a)(2) for purposes of section 82019, which defines "designated
employee." Mr. Ferreira would not be required to file an SEI unless he was a designated
employee. (Section 87302(b).)

Since Mr. Ferreira is a designated employee, he has not "permanently left state
service," and the revolving door prohibitions do not apply to Mr. Ferreira.

However, it is still necessary to examine whether Mr. Ferreira's employment
with multiple entities and his ownership of a business create economic interests that
present conflicts of interest that may prohibit him from acting as a consultant for
KCWA.

Conflict of Interest

The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) Pursuant to regulation 18700, an
eight-step analysis is applied to determine whether a public official has a conflict of
interest in a given governmental decision.

Step One: Is Mr. Ferreira a public official?

The conflict of interest prohibition applies only to public officials. As a
designated employee of the both CPA and SMUD, Mr. Ferreira is a "member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government agency" and, therefore, is a public
official subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048;
regulation 18701(a).)²

² It appears, based on the language of section III of the proposed contract between KCWA and Mr.
Ferreira, that KCWA, supra, does not intend Mr. Ferreira to be a "consultant" under that contract, but
intends him to be an independent contractor by subjecting his work to "intervening substantive review."
The title given to the person under contract is unimportant. It is the scope of activities that determines
whether that person is a "consultant" under the Act. (Gold Advice Letter, No. A-93-059.) We make no
determination on this issue, but note that "significant intervening substantive review" under regulation
18702.2(b) has been construed narrowly by the Commission for require more than the mere review of the
recommendations by supervisors, but rather the independent checking of the results without solely relying on
the data of the contractor. (Greenwald Advice Letter, No. I-90-349.) Further, a person under contract

File No. A-04-063
Page No. 7

Step Two: Is Mr. Ferreira making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?

Without material facts regarding a specific governmental decision, we are unable
to provide a formal conflict of interest analysis. However, we note that even if KCWA
determines that Mr. Ferreira is not a "consultant" for disclosure purposes because he does
not make or participate in making governmental decisions, he may still have
disqualification requirements if he influences those decisions.

The disclosure requirement is stated in section 87302(a), which states that each
agency shall, in its conflict of interest code, specifically enumerate:

"the positions within the agency . . . which involve the
making or participation in the making of decisions which
may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial
interest . . . ." (Emphasis added.)

For disqualification purposes, however, the requirement is much broader, as set
forth in section 87100:

"No public official at any level of state or local government
shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to
use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest." (Emphasis added.)

A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within
the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening
review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose
of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before,. . . any member,
officer, employee, or consultant of the agency." (Regulation 18703.(a).)

Step Three: What are Mr. Ferreira's "economic interests?"

The economic interests that might give rise to a conflict of interest are defined in
regulations 18703-18703.5. The specific economic interests that may apply to Mr.

participates in a decision even if his or her work is "reviewed" by several of his or her superiors, if those
superiors rely on the data or analysis prepared by the person without checking it independently, if they rely
on his judgment, or if he in some other way actually may influence the final decision. (Gold Advice Letter,
supra.)
3 Regulation 18702.4 lists a series of exceptions to the rules stated in the preceding three
regulations, which apply only in certain limited circumstances.

File No. A-04-063
Page No. 8

Ferreira are described below:

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a);
regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d);
regulation 18703.1(b)); and

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to
the decision (section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3).

Section 82030 defines "income," in pertinent part, as follows:

"(a) . . . Income of an individual also includes a pro rata
share of any income of any business entity or trust in which
the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater . . . .

"(b) 'Income' also does not include:

¶ . . . ¶

"(2) Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem
received from a state, local, or federal government agency
and reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem
received from a bona fide non-profit entity exempt from
taxation under section 501(c)(3) of the IRS Code."

As provided in section 82030(b)(2), salary and reimbursement for expenses or per
diem received from a state, local or federal agency is expressly exempted from the
definition of "income" for purposes of the Act. The Commission has concluded that,
normally, where a governmental entity pays compensation to a public official, the
compensation is not an economic interest that must be disclosed or will cause a conflict
of interest. (Egan Advice Letter, No. I-91-111; Cambra Advice Letter, No. I-92-245.)
Thus the compensation Mr. Ferreira receives from CPA and SMUD is not "income" for
purposes of section 82030. Consequently, financial effects from compensation received
from CPA and SMUD may be disregarded for purposes of section 87103(c) in his role as
a consultant or contractor to KCWA. (Lyon Advice Letter, No. A-88-391 (copy
enclosed).)

If KCWA determines that Mr. Ferreira is an independent contractor and not a
"consultant" under its conflict of interest code, with corresponding disclosure
requirements, then the income derived from the KCWA will not qualify for the
governmental salary exemption under section 82030(b)(2), and KCWA will be a source
of income to Mr. Ferreira under section 87103(c), which must be considered in any

File No. A-04-063
Page No. 9

conflict of interest analysis. However, if KCWA should determine that Mr. Ferreira is a
"consultant" and thus a designated employee under its conflict of interest code, any
income he receives from KCWA would be governmental salary and would be excluded
from the definition of "income" for purposes of any conflict of interest analysis.

Mr. Ferreira may also be precluded from participating in a governmental decision
under sections 87103(a) and (d). Mr. Ferreira has an ownership interest in a business
entity, Richard Ferreira, Inc. (Section 82005.) For purposes of this discussion, we
presume that Mr. Ferreira has an investment of $2,000 or more in his consulting firm.
Mr. Ferreira also holds a position of management in this business
entity. (Section 87103(d).) Thus, he may not participate in a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on his
consulting firm. For example, if his work for KCWA will result in additional income to
his firm from other water contractors, he may not participate in a governmental decision
if that additional income will have a material financial effect on the firm.

A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it
is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met as a result
of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be considered
"reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Regulation 18706(b) provides a non-exhaustive list
of relevant factors that should be considered in determining the foreseeability of a
material financial effect on an economic interest.

In order to determine whether a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest is material, it is necessary to determine if the
official's economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision. The standards for determining direct or indirect involvement are found in
regulations 18704 through 18704.5. The corresponding materiality standards are located
at regulations 18705 through 18705.5. To make a determination as to whether a specific
governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Mr.
Ferreira's economic interests, you should further consult these regulations.

2. May Mr. Ferreira provide consultation services to the KCWA concerning
the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission's relicensing process and
control of associated costs in connection with renewal of DWR's FERC
license for its Hyatt-Thermalito facility?

As with the first question, the revolving door provisions of the Act do not apply to
Mr. Ferreira, because he has not permanently left state service. Therefore, the only thing
under the Act that would preclude Mr. Ferreira from providing consultation services to the
KCWA concerning the FERC's relicensing process and control of associated costs in
connection with renewal of DWR's FERC license for its Hyatt-Thermalito facility, would
be a conflict of interest. We lack sufficient facts to undertake an analysis of this question.

File No. A-04-063
Page No. 10

We also remind you that there may be issues outside of the Act, such as issues
arising under Government Code § 1090 or the doctrine of incompatible offices, that may
require analysis. These issues are outside the jurisdiction of the Commission. We refer you
to the Attorney General's office on these matters.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Holly B. Armstrong
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure

1. Does the permanent ban on *switching sides'prevent Mr. Ferreira from
performing as a consultant and possibly as an expert witness for the Kern
County Water Agency in a dispute concerming the Department of \Mater
Resources' application of its guidelines for calculating revenues and costs
from its hydroelectric facilities, for purposes of billing its water
contractors, which were adopted as a result of the meetings of the
Committee on Energy of the Water Service Contractor's Council?
Revolvinq Door
Officials who leave state service are subject to two tlpes ofrestrictions under the
Act. The first is a permanent ban, and the second is a one-year prohibition, commonly
called the 'urevolving doo/'provisions of the Act.
Sections 87401 and87402 (collectively, the "permanent ban') prohibit former
state administrative officials from advising or representing any person for compensation in
any judicial or other proceeding in which the official participated while in state service.
(Sections 87 401 and 87 402.) Specifi cally, Section 87 401 provides:
'1lo former state administrative official, after the
termination of his or her employment or term of office,
shall for compensation act as agent or attomey for, or
otherwise represent, any other person (other than the State
of California) before any court or state administrative
agency or any officer or employee thereof by making any
formal or informal appearanoe, orbymaking any oral or
written communication with the intent to influencg in
connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding ifboth of the following apply:
"(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and
substantial interest.
*(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state
administrative official participated."
"'State administrative ofEcial' means every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state administrative agency who as part of his or her official
responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other than a
purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity." (Section 874000).)
Regulation 18741.1, in pertinent part, further clarifies:
"(a) The prohibitions of Govemment Code Sections 87401
ard87402 apply to any state administrative official if all of
the following criteria are met:
"(1) The official has permanently left state service or is on
a leave of absence."
Because Mr. Ferreira is required to file a Statement of Economic Interests by
CPA, we may conclude that CPA has determined that he is a "consultant," as defined in
regulation 18702.1(a)(2) for purposes of section 820l9,which defines "designated
employee." Mr. Ferreira would not be required to file an SEI unless he was a designated
employee. (Section 87 302(b).)
Since Mr. Ferreira is a designated employee, he has not "permane,lrtly left state
service," and the revolving door prohibitions do not apply to Mr. Femeira.
However, it is still necessary to examine whether Mr. Ferreira's employment
with multiple entities and his ownership of a business create economic interests that
present conflicts of interest that may prohibit him from acting as a consultant for
KCWA.
Conflict of fnterest
The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) Pursuant to regulation 18700, an
eight-step analysis is applied to determine whether a public official has a conflict of
interest in a given governmental decision.
Step One: Is Mr. Ferreira a public official?
The conflict of interest prohibition applies only to public officials. As a
designated ernployee of the both CPA and SMUD, Mr. Ferreira is a'?nernber, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local govemment ageirct''and, therefore, is a public
official subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048;
regulation I 870 I (a).)2
'It appears, based on the language of section III of the proposed contract between KCWA and Mr.
Ferreira, that KCWA, supra, does not intend Mr. Ferreira to be a 'tonsultant" under that contract, but
intends him to be an independent contractor by subjectrng his work to "intervening substantive review."
The title given to the person under contact is unimportant. It is the scope of activities that determines
whether that person is a "consultanf'under the Act. (Gold Adice ktter, No. A-93-059.) We make no
determination on this issue, but note that "significant intervening substantive review" under regulation
18702.2@) has been construed narrowly by the Commission to require more than the mere review of the
recommendations by superiors, but rather the independent checking of the results without solely relying on
the data of the contactor. (Greenwald AdviceLelter, No. I-90-349.) Further, a person under contract
step Two: Is Mr. Ferreira making, participating in making or influencing a
goYermmental decision?
Without material facts regarding a specific governmental decision, we are unable
to provide a formal conflict of interest analysis. However, we note that even if KCWA
determines that Mr. Ferreira is not a "consultant" for disclosure purposes because he does
not make orparticipate in making governmental decisions, he may still have
disqualification requirements if he influences those decisions.
The disclosure requirement is stated in section 87302(a), which states that each
agency shall, in its conflict of interest code, specifically enumerate:
"the positions within the agency . . . which involve the
making or participation in the making of decisions which
may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial
interest . . . ." (Emphasis added.)
For disqualification pu{poses, however, the requirement is much broader, as set
forth in section 87100:
'T.{o public official at arry level of state or local govemment
shall malre, partictpate in making or in any way attempt to
use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest." (Emphasis added.)
A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within
the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening
review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation I 5702.2.) A public 'bfficial is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision i{, for the purpose
of influencing the decision, the official contacts,^or appears before... any me,lnber,
officer, employee, or consultant of the agency."' (Regulation 18702.3(a).)
Step Three: What are Mr. tr'erreira's (economic interests?,
The economic interests that might give rise to a conflict of interest me defined in
regulations rc7a3-rc703.5. The specific economic interests that may apply to Mr.
participates in a decision even if his or her work is "reyiewed" by several of his or her superiors, if those
superiors rely on the data or analysis prepared by the person without checking it independently, if they rely
on his judgment, or if he in some other way actually may influence the final decision. (Gotd Advrce Letter,
supra.)
3 Regulation l87}2.4lists a series ofexceptions to the rules stated in the preceding three
regulations, which apply only in certain limited circumstances.
Ferreira are described below
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investrnent of $2,000 or more (section s7103(a);
regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, parhrer,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d);
regulation 1 8703. lO)); and
a
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 ormore within 12 months prior to
the decision (section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3).
Section 82A30 defines "incomg" in pertinentput, as follows:
a
"(a) . . . Income of an individual also includes a pro rata
share of any income of any business entity or trust in which
the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a lO-percent interest or greater. . . .
"(b) 'Income' also does not include:
T...fl
"(2) Salary and reimbursernent for expenses or per diem
received from a state, local, or federal government agency
and reimbursement for travel expenses and per diern
received from a bona fide non-profit entity exernpt from
taxation under section 501(cX3) of the IRS Code."
As provided in section 82030(bX2), salary and reimbursement for expenses or per
diem received from a state, local or federal agency is expressly exempted from the
definition of "income" forpurposes of the Act. The Commission has concluded that,
normallS where a govemmental enfitypays compensation to apublic official, the
compensation is not an eConomic interest that must be disclosed oi will catse a conflict
of interest. (Egan Advice Letter, No. I-91-1ll; Cambra Advice Letter, No.l-92-245.)
Thus the compensation Mr. Ferreira receives from CPA and SMUD is not "income" for
puq)oses of section 82030. Consequently, financial effects from compensation received
from CPA and SMUD maybe disregarded forpurposes of section S7103(c) in his role as
a consultant or contractor to KCWA. (Lyon Advice Letter, No. A-88-391 (copy
enclosed).)
If KCWA determines that Mr. Femeira is an independe,nt conkactor and not a
"consultant" under its conflict of interest code, with corresponding disclosure
requirernents, then the income derived from the KCWA will not qualiff for the
govemmental salary exemption under section 82030OX2), and KCWA will be a source
of income to Mr. Ferreira under section 87103(c), which must be considered in any
conflict of interest analysis. However, if KCWA should determine that Mr. Ferreira is a
"consultant" and thus a designated employee under its conflict of interest code, any
income he receives from KCWA would be govemmental salary and would be excluded
from the definition of "income" for purposes of any conflict of interest analysis.
Mr. Ferreira may also be precluded from participatingin agovernmental decision
under sections 87103(a) and (d). Mr. Ferreira has an ownership interest in a business
entity, Richard Ferreir4 Inc. (Section 82005.) For purposes of this discussion, we
presume that Mr. Ferreira has an investment of $2,000 or more in his consulting firm.
(Section 87103(a).) Mr. Ferreira also holds a position of rnanagement in this business
entity. (Section 87103(d).) Thus, he may not participate in a govemmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amatenalfinancial effect on his
consulting firm. For example, if his work for KCWA will result in additional income to
his firm from other water contractors, he may not participate in a govemmental decision
if that additional income will have a material financial effect on the firm.
A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it
is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met as a result
of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be
considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re
Thorner(1975) I FPPCOps. l9S.) Regulation 187060)providesanon-exhaustivelist
of relevant factors that should be considered in determining the foreseeability of a
material financial effect on an economic interest.
In order to determine whether a govemmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial ef,fect on an economic interest is material, it is necessary to determine if the
official's economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision. The standards for determining direct or indirect involvement are found in
regulations 18704 through 18704.5. The corresponding materiality standards are located
at regulations 18705 through 18705,.5. To make a determination as to whether a specific
governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Mr.
Ferreira's economic interests, you should further consult these regulations.
2. May Mr. Ferreira proyide consultation sefvices to thG KCWA concerning
the Federal Energy Regulatory Commissionrs relicensing process and
control of associated costs in connection with renewal of D'WRrs r'ERC
license for its Hyatt-Thermalito facility?
As with the first question, the revolving door provisions of the Act do not apply to
Mr. Ferreira, because he has not permanently left state se,lvice. Thereforg the only tiing
under the Act that would preclude Mr. Ferreira from providing consultation seryices to the
KCWA conceming the FERC's relicensing process and control of associated costs in
connection with renewal of DWR's FERC license for its Hyatt-Thermalito facility, would
be a conflict of interest. We lack sufficient facts to undertake an analysis of this question.


Page No. l0
We also remind you that there may be issues outside of the Act, such as issues
arising under Government Code $ 1090 or the doctrine of incompatible offices , thatmay
require analysis. These issues are outside the jurisdiction of the Commission. We refer you
to the Attorney General's office on these maffers.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. 
Does the permanent ban on "switching sides" prevent Mr. Ferreira from
performing as a consultant and possibly as an expert witness for the Kern County Water
Agency ("KCWA') in a dispute concerning the Department of Water Resources'
("DWR') application of its guidelines for calculating revenues and costs from its
hydroeleckic facilities, for purposes of billing its water contractors, which were adopted
as a result of the meetings of the Committee on Energy of the Water Service Conkactor,s
Council ("Committee')?
2. 
May Mr. Ferreira provide consultation services to the KCWA conceming
the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission's ("FERC') relicensing process and controi
of associated costs in connection with renewal of DWR's FERC license for its Hyatt-
Thermalito facility?
I Government Code sections 8 1000 - gl}14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
iffii;l

CONCLUSION: 1 &2. Because Mr. Ferreira is still employed as a consultant and under a conhact with
the California Consumer Power and Conservation Financing Authority, and is a
designated employee under its conflict of interest codq he has not permanently left state
service. Therefore, the post-employment provisions of the Act do not preclude Mr.
Ferreira from working as a consultant to KCWA. However, there may be conflict of
interest issues with respect to specific governmental decisions, depending on whether
KCWA determines that Mr. Ferreira is a "consultant" forpurposes of disclosure under
the Act.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 May 4, 2004 Steven G. Churchwell Livingston & Mattesich 1201 K Street, Suite 1100 Sacramento, CA 95814-3938 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-04-063 Dear Mr. Churchwell: This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Richard Ferreira for advice regarding the post-governmental employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We encourage you to consider whether other laws, such as Government Code 1090 or the doctrine of incompatible offices, may also be implicated by your situation. The Commission is charged with interpreting and enforcing the provisions of the Act, and may provide advice only with respect to those provisions. (Section 83114.) Our advice is based on the facts presented in your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS 1. Does the permanent ban on "switching sides" prevent Mr. Ferreira from performing as a consultant and possibly as an expert witness for the Kern County Water Agency ("KCWA") in a dispute concerning the Department of Water Resources' ("DWR") application of its guidelines for calculating revenues and costs from its hydroelectric facilities, for purposes of billing its water contractors, which were adopted as a result of the meetings of the Committee on Energy of the Water Service Contractor's Council ("Committee")? 2. May Mr. Ferreira provide consultation services to the KCWA concerning the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission's ("FERC") relicensing process and control of associated costs in connection with renewal of DWR's FERC license for its Hyatt-Thermalito facility? _______________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-04-063 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION 1 & 2. Because Mr. Ferreira is still employed as a consultant and under a contract with the California Consumer Power and Conservation Financing Authority, and is a designated employee under its conflict of interest code, he has not permanently left state service. Therefore, the post-employment provisions of the Act do not preclude Mr. Ferreira from working as a consultant to KCWA. However, there may be conflict of interest issues with respect to specific governmental decisions, depending on whether KCWA determines that Mr. Ferreira is a "consultant" for purposes of disclosure under the Act. FACTS Mr. Ferreira was employed by the Department of Water Resources ("DWR") from 1964 to 1986. The Kern County Water Agency ("KCWA") would like to hire Mr. Ferreira as a consultant and potential expert witness. Mr. Ferreira's positions and duties with the DWR were as follows: February 1964 to April 1976 – Electric Utilities Engineer. Supervised and performed various engineering, economic and financial studies related to generation and transmission planning and development. May 1976 to April 1982 – Chief, Renewable Resource Development Branch. Responsible for the permitting, licensing, management and administration of alternative resource development, including hydroelectric, wind, solar and
